reviewed.by: deity-of-Christ reel argued historically from Mark via the enemies'-reaction method (two blasphemy scenes: forgive on earth, Mark 2:7; judge from heaven, Mark 14:62). Grounded in the certified library/jesus_as_god_nt.html + library/jesus_claims.html and the citations-gated Del Rosario reading-club demo. Positive-case-only (no objections voiced). Calibration held: 'best explanation' not 'proof'; divine authority / 'what belongs to God alone' (divine-identity), not a second/lesser deity. GATES: apologia-argument + apologia-orthodoxy.

voiceover: Did Jesus ever actually say he was God? One of the strongest answers doesn't start with John's famous 'I am' sayings. It starts with Mark, the earliest Gospel, and with the reaction of Jesus' own enemies. Scene one, Mark chapter two: a paralyzed man is lowered through a roof, and Jesus tells him his sins are forgiven. The religious scholars are stunned. Who can forgive sins, they say, but God alone? Forgiving sins was God's own prerogative, and Jesus had just claimed it. Scene two, Mark chapter fourteen: on trial for his life, the high priest asks, are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed? Jesus answers in the words of Daniel and the Psalms: the Son of Man, seated at the right hand of Power, coming on the clouds of heaven. The high priest tears his robes and cries blasphemy. Put the two scenes together: authority to forgive on earth, and authority to judge from heaven, a place that belongs to God alone. Jesus never needed the words 'I am God.' His enemies understood exactly what he was claiming. And the best explanation of the earliest evidence is not that the church invented his divinity later. He claimed it from the start.
source: library/jesus_as_god_nt.html + library/jesus_claims.html (deity of Christ from Mark; enemies'-reaction method) — Mark 2:1-12; 14:53-65

sub.t: Don’t start with John. Start with his enemies.

scenes.kicker: THE HIGH PRIEST TEARS HIS ROBES

sub.t: The best explanation of the earliest evidence.
